title: JavaScript Advanced Concepts
description: Deep dive into closures, event loop, and advanced JS mechanics essential for senior roles.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a microtask in the JavaScript Event Loop?
What is the purpose of the `Proxy` object introduced in ES6?

options.text: To create a deep clone of an object.
To intercept and redefine fundamental operations for an object.
To proxy network requests in the browser.

questions.questionText: What is a 'closure' in JavaScript?

options.text: A function bundled with its lexical environment.
A way to close over variables to make them private.
A method to avoid memory leaks.
A design pattern for creating singletons.

questions.questionText: What is the result of `typeof null`?
How does `Object.freeze()` differ from `Object.seal()`?

options.text: freeze prevents adding properties, seal prevents deleting.
freeze prevents modification of values; seal only prevents addition/deletion.
freeze makes the object immutable recursively.

questions.questionText: What does the `finally` block in a Promise chain guarantee?

options.text: It runs only if the promise resolves.
It runs only if the promise rejects.
It runs regardless of the promise outcome.

questions.questionText: Which operator is used for optional chaining?
What is the output of `[1, 2, 3].map(parseInt)`?
What is the primary characteristic of `WeakMap`?

options.text: Keys must be objects and are weakly referenced.

questions.questionText: What is 'Temporal Dead Zone' (TDZ)?

options.text: The time between engine start and DOM ready.
The period between entering scope and variable declaration for let/const.
The zone where memory leaks occur.

questions.questionText: How can you deep clone an object containing nested objects, Dates, and RegExps?
What does `Symbol.iterator` define?

options.text: The default iterator for an object.
A unique key for object properties.
A method to get symbol descriptions.
A way to enumerate private fields.

questions.questionText: What is the execution context of an arrow function?

options.text: It inherits `this` from the enclosing lexical context.
It throws an error when accessing `this`.
It binds `this` to the global object.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes the difference between `async/await` and raw `Promises`?

options.text: async/await is a different concurrency model.
async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises with better error handling using try/catch.
Promises are non-blocking; async/await is blocking.
async/await cannot be used in loops.

title: Advanced React Patterns & Performance
description: Expert-level questions on HOCs, Render Props, Hooks, and React performance optimization.

questions.questionText: What is the primary advantage of using a 'Compound Component' pattern?

options.text: Implicit state sharing and flexible markup structure.

questions.questionText: If a parent component re-renders, under what condition will a child component wrapped in `React.memo` skip re-rendering?

options.text: If the child is a class component.
If all props are primitive values.
If the props reference equality remains the same.

questions.questionText: What is the main use case for the `useImperativeHandle` hook?

options.text: To force a component to re-render.
To customize the instance value exposed when using `ref`.

questions.questionText: In React 18 Concurrent Features, what does `useTransition` allow you to do?
What is a common pitfall when using the `key` prop with `React.Fragment`?

options.text: The short syntax `<>` cannot accept keys.
Keys on fragments cause memory leaks.

questions.questionText: Which approach is recommended for lifting state up efficiently to avoid unnecessary re-renders?

options.text: Using Context API for all shared state.
Using a state management library like Redux for everything.
Lifting state to the nearest common ancestor and memoizing children.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `useLayoutEffect` hook?

options.text: It runs before the browser paints, synchronously after DOM mutations.
It runs after the browser paints.

questions.questionText: How can you prevent a function prop from causing unnecessary re-renders in a memoized child?

options.text: Define the function inside the child.
Bind the function in the render method.
Wrap the function with `useCallback` in the parent.

questions.questionText: What is an error boundary in React?
In which scenario would you choose `useReducer` over `useState`?

options.text: Complex state logic involving multiple sub-values or transitions.

questions.questionText: What is the significance of 'Strict Mode' in React 18 regarding `useEffect`?

options.text: It disables all effects in production.
It double-invokes effects in development to detect side-effects.

questions.questionText: What does the `useDeferredValue` hook do?

options.text: Defers the value update to keep the UI responsive.
Defers the execution of a callback.

questions.questionText: Why are inline styles discouraged for performance-critical dynamic styling?

options.text: They create new objects on every render, breaking memoization.
They cannot be parsed by PostCSS.
They are not supported in Server Components.

questions.questionText: What is the core principle behind the 'Container/Presentational' pattern?

options.text: Mixing logic and markup in one component.
Separating logic (how things work) from presentation (how things look).

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to share non-UI logic between components?

title: Next.js App Router & Server Components
description: Comprehensive test on Next.js 13/14+ concepts including Server Components, Streaming, and Caching.

questions.questionText: What is the default behavior of components in the Next.js App Router?
Which directive is used to explicitly mark a component as a Client Component?
How can you pass data from a Server Component to a Client Component?

options.text: Serializing the data to JSON and passing via props.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `loading.js` file in a route segment?

options.text: To define the initial state of the page.
To create an instant loading state UI using Suspense.
To load data for the layout.

questions.questionText: Which function is recommended for fetching data in Server Components?

options.text: async/await fetch directly in the component body.

questions.questionText: What is 'Partial Prerendering' (PPR)?

options.text: Rendering only the visible part of the page.
Combining static and dynamic components in the same initial response.
Prerendering pages partially based on user roles.

questions.questionText: What is the role of the `next.config.js` `reactStrictMode` option?

options.text: It is unrelated to Next.js specific features.
It identifies unsafe lifecycles and legacy API usage.

questions.questionText: How does `next/link` prefetch routes by default in App Router?

options.text: Prefetches all routes on initial visit.
Prefetches only static routes in production.
Prefetches routes when they become visible in the viewport.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `next/dynamic` when used in App Router?
Where do you define shared metadata for all pages (e.g., title template)?
What does the `revalidatePath` function do?

options.text: Invalidates the router cache for a specific path on-demand.
Triggers a rebuild of the entire site.

questions.questionText: Which feature prevents the entire page from blocking on slow data fetches?
What is the correct way to handle redirects in a Server Component?
What is the significance of the `'use server'` directive?

options.text: It marks a file to be executed only on the server (React Server Actions).

questions.questionText: Which caching mechanism is new in Next.js 14 that affects `fetch`?

options.text: The Full Route Cache and Router Cache.

title: TypeScript Advanced Types & Generics
description: Mapped Types, Conditional Types, and advanced TypeScript techniques for robust type safety.

questions.questionText: What does the `keyof` operator produce?

options.text: A union of values of the object.
A union of property names of a type.
The prototype chain of an object.

questions.questionText: How can you make all properties of `T` optional and readonly?

options.text: Both 1 and 2 produce the same result.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `infer` keyword?

options.text: To infer the return type of a function.
To infer the type of a variable in a conditional type clause.
To declare a variable inside a type.

questions.questionText: Given `type Getters<T> = { [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K] };`, what does this mapped type do?

options.text: Creates new properties by prefixing keys with 'get' and turning them to functions.
Creates a strict set of action creators.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `interface` and `type` regarding declaration merging?

options.text: Interfaces can be merged; types cannot.
Types can be merged; interfaces cannot.

questions.questionText: What does the `NonNullable<T>` utility type do?
How does `const` assertion (`as const`) affect a literal object?

options.text: It makes the object deeply immutable and infers the most specific literal types.
It prevents the object from being copied.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Discriminated Union'?

options.text: A union of types that all share a common literal property used to narrow them.
A union that is represented as a string enum.
A type that cannot be instantiated.
A union of all string literals in a codebase.

questions.questionText: When would you use `unknown` over `any`?

options.text: When you don't know the type and want to enforce type checking before usage.
When you want to disable type checking entirely.
When dealing with third-party libraries exclusively.

questions.questionText: What is 'Excess Property Checking'?

options.text: Validation of the number of props passed to a component.
TypeScript erroring when an object literal has properties not defined in the target type.

questions.questionText: What does the `-?` modifier do in mapped types?

options.text: Converts a number to a string.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `Awaited<T>`?

options.text: To unwrap a Promise type recursively.
To mark a value for top-level await.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes 'Contravariance' in function parameter types?

options.text: A function accepting a parent class can be assigned to a function accepting a child class.
A function accepting a child class can be assigned to a function accepting a parent class.
Function parameters are bivariant in TypeScript by default.
TypeScript has strict variance like C#.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Branded Type' (Nominal Typing) used for?

options.text: To distinguish two types with the same structure (e.g., UserId vs PostId).
To add metadata to runtime values.
To enforce brand colors in styling.

questions.questionText: What does the `satisfies` operator do?

options.text: Checks that an expression matches a type without changing the inferred type.
Casts an expression to a specific type.
Asserts that a value is not null.

title: Backend Development: Node.js & Express
description: Master the core concepts of building scalable APIs with Node.js and Express.

questions.questionText: What is the correct order of execution for middleware in Express?
How do you pass control to the next middleware function in the stack?

options.text: All of the above depending on context.

questions.questionText: What is the Event Loop in Node.js?

options.text: A loop that iterates over event listeners.
A mechanism that handles asynchronous callbacks in a single thread.
A queue for processing HTTP requests.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Buffer' class used for in Node.js?

options.text: To allocate memory in V8 heap.

questions.questionText: How can you prevent blocking the Event Loop with CPU-intensive tasks?

options.text: Offloading to Worker Threads or child processes.

questions.questionText: What does `cluster` module allow you to do?

options.text: Create child processes that share server ports.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `helmet` middleware in Express?

options.text: To secure Express apps by setting various HTTP headers.

questions.questionText: What is 'CORS' and how is it typically handled in Express?

options.text: It's a security feature; use `cors` middleware.

questions.questionText: What is 'Stream' in Node.js?

options.text: A way to handle large data piece by piece without buffering entirely.

questions.questionText: Which statement is true regarding `process.env`?
What is the correct way to handle uncaught exceptions in a Node.js application?

options.text: They cannot be caught; Node exits.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `res.send()` and `res.json()` in Express?

options.text: `res.json` ensures the `Content-Type` is `application/json` and converts objects.

questions.questionText: When does a memory leak occur in Node.js?

options.text: When objects are no longer referenced.
When memory is allocated but not released due to lingering references.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of using `app.route()` in Express?

options.text: To chain route handlers for a single path.

questions.questionText: Which package is commonly used for environment variable management?

title: REST API Design & Best Practices
description: Ensure your API design is scalable, intuitive, and follows industry standards.

questions.questionText: Which HTTP method is idempotent but NOT safe?
What is the correct status code for a successful POST request that creates a resource?
How should you represent filtering, sorting, and pagination in a RESTful URL?

options.text: Using JSON in the request body with GET.

questions.questionText: What is HATEOAS?

options.text: Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to version an API?

options.text: All of the above are valid approaches.

questions.questionText: What status code indicates 'Precondition Failed' for optimistic concurrency?
Which HTTP method should be used for a partial update of a resource?
What does the '429 Too Many Requests' status code indicate?

options.text: The user has exceeded rate limits.
The request payload is too large.
The request method is not allowed.

questions.questionText: What is the 'JSON:API' specification primarily concerned with?
Which HTTP verb is considered 'Safe' according to the HTTP specification?
When should you use nouns (resources) vs verbs in REST URLs?

options.text: Always use nouns; resources are things.
Verbs are fine for non-CRUD operations (`/search`).

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `Cache-Control: no-cache` header?

options.text: Prevents the response from being stored.
Allows caching but requires revalidation with origin server before use.

questions.questionText: What is an 'Idempotency Key' used for?

options.text: To safely retry non-idempotent requests like POST without duplication.

questions.questionText: Which header is used for 'Content Negotiation'?
What is the best practice for error responses?

options.text: Return stack traces for easy debugging.
Use consistent envelope with `code`, `message`, and optionally `details`.
Use different structures for 4xx and 5xx errors.

title: GraphQL Concepts & Schema Design
description: Understanding GraphQL schemas, resolvers, and best practices for modern API layers.

questions.questionText: What is the primary difference between GraphQL and REST regarding data fetching?

options.text: GraphQL allows clients to specify exactly what data they need.

questions.questionText: What are the three core operation types in a GraphQL schema?
What is the 'N+1' problem in GraphQL and how is it typically solved?

options.text: Too many resolver calls; solved with Dataloader batching.
Too many mutations; solved with transactions.
Large query complexity; solved with depth limits.

questions.questionText: Which directive is used to deprecate a field in a GraphQL schema?
What is the purpose of a 'Resolver' in GraphQL?

options.text: To provide the logic for fetching the data for a specific field.
To parse the incoming HTTP request.

questions.questionText: What is an 'Input Type' used for?

options.text: To define the structure of arguments for mutations.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Fragment' in GraphQL queries?

options.text: A set of fields reused across multiple queries.
An incomplete response from the server.
A piece of a union type.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to handle errors in GraphQL?

options.text: Use the `errors` array alongside partial data.
Return custom error objects in the data field.

questions.questionText: What is Schema Stitching / Federation?

options.text: Merging multiple GraphQL services into a single unified graph.
Splitting a monolithic schema into smaller files.

questions.questionText: What does the `__typename` field represent?

options.text: The name of the object type being resolved.
The name of the query operation.

questions.questionText: What is a downside of GraphQL compared to REST?

options.text: Complexity of caching and file uploads.

questions.questionText: What does the `@connection` directive in Apollo do?

options.text: Manages pagination and cache key normalization for lists.

questions.questionText: What is the type of the root object in a Subscription resolver?
What is the correct way to define an Interface in GraphQL?
How can a client request real-time data from a GraphQL server?

title: State Management: Redux & Context
description: Comparing Context API, Redux Toolkit, Zustand, and managing global state effectively.

questions.questionText: What is the primary difference between the Context API and Redux regarding rendering behavior?

options.text: Redux subscribes to store, Context re-renders all consumers on any value change.
Context is better for frequent updates.
Redux cannot be used with functional components.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `createSlice` in Redux Toolkit?

options.text: To generate actions and reducers automatically using Immer.
To split the Redux store into multiple files.

questions.questionText: Which principle states that state should be normalized (flat) in Redux?

options.text: Changes are made with Pure Reducers.

questions.questionText: In Zustand, what is a key advantage over Redux Toolkit?

options.text: Minimal boilerplate and no provider wrapping required.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Selector' function in Redux?

options.text: A function that picks which action to dispatch.
A function that computes derived data from the store.
A component that selects the provider.

questions.questionText: How can you optimize a React component that uses `useContext` to prevent unnecessary re-renders?

options.text: Memoize the component and split context providers.
It is impossible; switch to Redux.

questions.questionText: What is 'Redux Middleware' primarily used for?

options.text: Extending Redux with custom functionality like logging, crash reporting, async logic.

questions.questionText: What is 'RTK Query'?

options.text: A data fetching and caching solution built into Redux Toolkit.

questions.questionText: When is 'Lifting State Up' the preferred approach over global state?

options.text: When state is shared by a few sibling components.
When state is needed across many route pages.
When state involves complex async workflows.
When you need to persist state.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Reducer' conceptually?

options.text: A function to reduce array length.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to handle async logic in Redux Toolkit?
What is the main concern when using Jotai or Recoil compared to Redux?

options.text: Atomic state management prevents 'prop drilling' and optimizes re-renders.
They are not suitable for production.
They do not work with React 18.

questions.questionText: What does the `immer` library (used in Redux Toolkit) allow you to do?

options.text: Write mutable code that produces immutable updates.

questions.questionText: What is 'State Hydration' in the context of SSR (Server-Side Rendering)?

options.text: Synchronizing the server-rendered state with the client app.

questions.questionText: In Redux, why does combining reducers require careful attention to state slice keys?

options.text: Because the combined reducer maps keys to slices of state.
Because only one reducer can run.
Because they share the same action types.

title: Web Performance Metrics & Optimization
description: Master Core Web Vitals, bundle optimization, and rendering performance.

questions.questionText: What are the three Core Web Vitals?
What does 'Code Splitting' achieve?

options.text: Smaller initial JavaScript bundles by lazy-loading.

questions.questionText: What is 'Tree Shaking'?

options.text: Eliminating dead code from the final bundle.

questions.questionText: What layout CSS property triggers the least amount of browser layout recalculations?
What is the purpose of the `loading='lazy'` attribute?

options.text: To defer loading of images/iframes until they are near the viewport.

questions.questionText: What is 'First Contentful Paint' (FCP)?

options.text: Time when the page becomes fully interactive.
Time when the browser renders the first piece of DOM content.
Time when the largest image loads.
Time the HTML document is received.

questions.questionText: What does the `will-change` CSS property do?

options.text: Warns the browser of upcoming changes to optimize rendering layers.

questions.questionText: How does Brotli compare to Gzip generally?

options.text: Gzip is significantly faster at decoding.
Brotli is supported only on HTTPS.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Critical Request Chain' in Lighthouse?

options.text: The sequence of dependent network requests that block render.
A list of failed XHR requests.

questions.questionText: Which technique helps prevent 'Cumulative Layout Shift' (CLS)?
What is 'Largest Contentful Paint' (LCP) threshold for 'Good'?
What does `type="module"` on a script tag enable?
What is the benefit of `link rel='preload'`?

options.text: Early loading of critical resources before they are discovered by parser.

questions.questionText: What is the primary goal of reducing 'Time to Interactive' (TTI)?

options.text: To make the page respond to user input quickly.
To display the first pixel fast.

questions.questionText: Which React API helps avoid expensive calculations on every render?

title: Web Security: XSS, CSRF & Best Practices
description: Critical security concepts every frontend/backend developer must know.

questions.questionText: What is the most effective way to prevent XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks in React?
Which HTTP header helps prevent MIME type sniffing?
How does a CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attack work?

options.text: Tricking the browser to execute a malicious script.
Tricking a user's browser to send a forged request to a site they're logged into.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `SameSite` cookie attribute?

options.text: To restrict cookies to first-party context.
To make cookies accessible only via HTTPS.

questions.questionText: Where is it safest to store a JWT token in a single-page application?
What is 'Content Security Policy' (CSP) used for?

options.text: Preventing XSS and data injection attacks.

questions.questionText: What is 'SQL Injection' and how can it be prevented in Node.js?

options.text: Using parameterized queries with an ORM/ODM.
Using regex to allow only alpha-numeric chars.

questions.questionText: What is 'Clickjacking' and how is it prevented?
What is the main security concern with `eval()` in JavaScript?
How does 'Subresource Integrity' (SRI) help?

options.text: Ensures a third-party script hasn't been tampered with.

questions.questionText: What is an 'Open Redirect' vulnerability?

options.text: Redirecting users to an arbitrary external URL using user input.

questions.questionText: Which library helps sanitize HTML in Node.js to prevent XSS?
What is 'CORS'?
What is the purpose of Rate Limiting?

options.text: Prevent brute-force attacks and resource exhaustion.

questions.questionText: Why is `strict-transport-security` (HSTS) header important?

options.text: Forces browsers to always use HTTPS for the domain.

title: Testing Strategies: Unit, Integration, E2E
description: Master Jest, React Testing Library, and Cypress for robust frontend testing.

questions.questionText: What is the primary goal of 'Unit Testing'?

options.text: Testing the smallest piece of logic in isolation.

questions.questionText: Which query should you use first according to React Testing Library guiding principles?
What is a 'Mock' in unit testing?

options.text: A duplicated version of the production code.
A simulated object that mimics real behavior and tracks calls.

questions.questionText: In Jest, what does `beforeEach` do?

options.text: Runs before every test in a describe block.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `act` and `waitFor` in React Testing Library?

options.text: `act` flushes immediate sync updates; `waitFor` waits for async updates.
`waitFor` is used with DOM events only.

questions.questionText: What is 'Code Coverage' in testing?

options.text: Percentage of code executed during tests.
Performance report of the test suite.

questions.questionText: When writing an E2E test with Cypress, what is the typical interaction pattern?
What is 'Snapshot Testing' primarily used for?
How can you test an API call in a React component without hitting the real network?

options.text: Mock the fetch function using Jest.

questions.questionText: What does the 'Arrange-Act-Assert' (AAA) pattern mean?

options.text: Setup test data -> Execute action -> Verify result.

questions.questionText: What is `jest.spyOn(object, methodName)` used for?

options.text: Tracking calls to a method without overwriting implementation.
Modifying the source code at runtime.

questions.questionText: What is the main benefit of 'Test-Driven Development' (TDD)?

options.text: Ensuring code meets requirements and better design.

questions.questionText: How does `jest.mock('../path', () => ({ __esModule: true, default: mockFn }))` differ from manual global mocks?

options.text: It's an inline auto-mock that hoists to the top of the file.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `data-testid` attribute?

options.text: To provide a stable selector for tests unrelated to styling/content.
To organize code by test ID.

questions.questionText: What is a common challenge when running E2E tests in a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Flakiness due to network or timing issues.

title: Material UI & Modern Styling
description: Deep dive into MUI theming, customization, and CSS-in-JS performance implications.

questions.questionText: What is the primary styling engine used in Material UI v5?
How do you customize a component's styles globally in MUI?

options.text: Using the `sx` prop on every instance.

questions.questionText: What does the `sx` prop provide?

options.text: A superset of CSS with access to the theme.

questions.questionText: What is the key difference between `styled` API from MUI system and `emotion/styled`?

options.text: MUI `styled` automatically attaches `className` for overrides and theme access.
MUI version only works with inline styles.

questions.questionText: How can you reduce the CSS bundle size when using MUI?
What is the purpose of `CssBaseline`?

options.text: Normalizing CSS across browsers and applying baseline Material styles.

questions.questionText: How does MUI handle responsive styles with the `sx` prop?

options.text: Using `@media` queries only in CSS files.
It does not support responsive sx.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to apply dark mode in MUI?
Which component is suitable for creating complex flex/grid layouts rapidly?
What is the difference between `Container` and `Box`?

options.text: Container centers content horizontally with padding; Box is a generic low-level wrapper.
Container holds state; Box does not.
Container has a border; Box does not.
They are identical in MUI v5.

questions.questionText: What is 'CSS injection order' issue in MUI and how to fix it?

options.text: Use `<StyledEngineProvider injectFirst>` to ensure MUI styles load before Tailwind.

questions.questionText: Which hook provides direct access to the theme object inside a component?
How can you extend the Material UI theme with custom properties?
What is the best practice for handling spacing in MUI?
What is the purpose of `GlobalStyles` in MUI?

options.text: To apply baseline or global resets using the theme.

title: Microservices Architecture Patterns
description: Understanding distributed systems, communication, and resilience patterns.

questions.questionText: What is an 'API Gateway' primarily used for?

options.text: Single entry point for routing, auth, aggregation, and protocol translation.

questions.questionText: Which pattern helps prevent cascading failures in a microservices ecosystem?
What is the 'Saga' pattern used for?
What is the main characteristic of 'Event-Driven Architecture'?

options.text: Services communicate by producing and consuming events.

questions.questionText: What does 'CQRS' stand for and what is its benefit?

options.text: Command Query Responsibility Segregation; separating read/write models for scalability.
Client Queue Registry Service; improving latency.
Common Quality Restriction Service; ensuring standards.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Dead Letter Queue' (DLQ)?

options.text: A queue for messages that cannot be processed successfully.
A queue that delivers messages once.

questions.questionText: Which container orchestration platform is most common for microservices?
What is 'Service Discovery'?

options.text: Finding the IP/Port of a service instance dynamically.

questions.questionText: Which protocol is lighter and faster for internal microservice communication than HTTP/REST?
What is 'Database per Service' pattern?

options.text: Each microservice owns its private database/tables.
All services share one read replica.
One service manages all DB transactions.

questions.questionText: What is the role of a 'Message Broker' (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka)?
What is 'Observability' comprised of?
What is a 'Configuration Server' in Spring Cloud (or similar)?

options.text: Centralized external configuration for all microservices.

questions.questionText: What does 'Idempotency' mean in a distributed system?

options.text: A request can be called multiple times without changing the result beyond the initial application.
A request returns the same result every time.

questions.questionText: What is a key disadvantage of Microservices compared to Monoliths?

options.text: Increased operational complexity and network latency.
Slower development speed on small teams.

title: Docker & Containerization
description: Essential Dockerfile commands, multi-stage builds, and container orchestration basics.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between a Docker Image and a Container?

options.text: Image is a read-only template; Container is a runnable instance.
Image is stored in DockerHub; Container is local.
Container is read-only; Image is writable.

questions.questionText: What does the `COPY` instruction do in a Dockerfile?

options.text: Copies files from host to image filesystem.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of 'Multi-Stage Builds'?

options.text: To reduce final image size by discarding intermediate build dependencies.
To run multiple services in one container.
To speed up the build cache.

questions.questionText: What does `EXPOSE 3000` do?

options.text: Documents that the container listens on port 3000.
Opens port 3000 to the host automatically.

questions.questionText: How do you persist data generated by a container?
What is the purpose of a `.dockerignore` file?

options.text: To prevent sensitive or large files from being sent to the build context.
To list files that should not be copied.

questions.questionText: Which command is used to run a container in detached mode?
What does `docker-compose.yml` primarily define?

options.text: Multi-container Docker applications and their relationships.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `CMD` and `ENTRYPOINT`?

options.text: CMD provides defaults; ENTRYPOINT defines the executable container runs.
CMD is for arguments; ENTRYPOINT is for environment.

questions.questionText: What is an 'Image Layer'?

options.text: Read-only filesystem diff created by each Dockerfile step.

questions.questionText: How can you execute a command inside a running container?
What is a best practice for reducing Docker image size?

options.text: Cleaning up package manager caches in the same RUN layer.
Using multiple `RUN` commands for each package.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of 'Docker Swarm'?

options.text: Native clustering and orchestration for Docker.

questions.questionText: What network mode allows a container to use the host's network stack directly?
What is the `HEALTHCHECK` instruction used for?

options.text: To allow Docker to monitor if a container is still working.

title: CI/CD & Advanced Git Workflows
description: Master GitHub Actions, Git Flow, rebasing, and modern deployment pipelines.

questions.questionText: What is 'Continuous Integration' (CI)?

options.text: Automatically merging and testing code changes frequently.
Writing code on a single branch.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `git merge` and `git rebase`?

options.text: Merge preserves history; Rebase rewrites history linearly.

questions.questionText: What is the 'Golden Rule' of `git rebase`?

options.text: Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch.
Only rebase feature branches onto release.
Rebase is not safe to use.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Git Hook'?

options.text: Scripts that run automatically on specific events (pre-commit, post-receive).
An alias for a Git command.

questions.questionText: In GitHub Actions, what is a 'Runner'?

options.text: The server that executes the workflow jobs.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `.github/workflows/main.yml`?
What does `git cherry-pick <hash>` do?

options.text: Applies changes from a specific commit to the current branch.
Creates a tag on a commit.

questions.questionText: What is 'Blue-Green Deployment'?

options.text: Running two identical production environments and switching traffic.

questions.questionText: What is 'Trunk-Based Development'?

options.text: Developers merge small, frequent changes into main branch.
Using a single commit per sprint.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `git reflog`?

options.text: Recover lost commits or undo accidental resets.
Log all repository events for compliance.

questions.questionText: What is 'Semantic Versioning' (SemVer) format?
What is the primary benefit of 'Infrastructure as Code' (IaC) in CI/CD?
What does `git bisect` do?

options.text: Binary search through commit history to find a bug.

questions.questionText: What is an 'Artifact' in a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Files generated during the build (e.g., binaries, test reports).

questions.questionText: What is the main function of `webpack` in a modern CI pipeline?

options.text: To bundle and optimize assets as part of the build step.

title: Authentication & Authorization
description: Deep understanding of OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, JWT, and session security.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `Authorization Code` grant type in OAuth 2.0?

options.text: Secure exchange for server-side apps where client secret is kept safe.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?

options.text: AuthN verifies who you are; AuthZ verifies what you can do.
AuthN is for APIs; AuthZ is for UIs.
AuthN uses JWT; AuthZ uses Cookies.

questions.questionText: Which part of a JWT contains the signature?

options.text: The third segment after the second dot.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Refresh Token' used for?

options.text: To obtain a new Access Token without re-authenticating the user.

questions.questionText: What is 'OpenID Connect' (OIDC)?

options.text: An identity layer built on top of OAuth 2.0.

questions.questionText: Why should JWT tokens avoid storing sensitive data in the payload?

options.text: The payload is only base64-encoded, not encrypted by default.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of `HttpOnly` cookies for authentication?

options.text: Prevents client-side JavaScript from reading the token.

questions.questionText: What is 'Role-Based Access Control' (RBAC)?

options.text: Permissions assigned to roles rather than individual users.
A method to compress API responses.

questions.questionText: What does `state` parameter in OAuth 2.0 protect against?
What is 'Multi-Factor Authentication' (MFA)?

options.text: Using more than one method to verify identity.

questions.questionText: What algorithm is used for the JWT signature if the token starts with `eyJ...` and is signed symmetrically?
What is a common method to revoke JWT tokens since they are stateless?

options.text: Using a token blocklist/denylist on the server.
Waiting for the token to expire.

questions.questionText: What is 'PKCE' (Proof Key for Code Exchange)?

options.text: Security extension for OAuth mobile/native apps.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `bcrypt` and `crypto` modules in Node.js?

options.text: Bcrypt is for password hashing with salt; crypto is general-purpose.
Bcrypt is built into Node core.

questions.questionText: What is 'Session Fixation'?

options.text: Attacker tricks user to use a known session ID.

title: Functional Programming in JavaScript
description: Currying, composition, functors, monads, and writing declarative code.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Pure Function'?

options.text: A function with no side effects and deterministic output.
A function that returns a string.

questions.questionText: What is 'Currying'?

options.text: Transforming a function with multiple args into a sequence of functions each taking one arg.
A way to cache function results.

questions.questionText: What does `compose` (or `pipe`) do?

options.text: Combines functions where output of one is input to next.

questions.questionText: What is 'Point-Free Style'?

options.text: Defining functions without explicitly mentioning the arguments.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Functor'?

options.text: A container that implements a `map` method.
A function that returns a function.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Monad'?
How does `Array.prototype.reduce` relate to functional programming?

options.text: It embodies the concept of fold/catamorphism.

questions.questionText: What is 'Referential Transparency'?

options.text: An expression can be replaced with its value without changing program behavior.
A function exposes its source code.
A function is defined with a `function` keyword.

questions.questionText: What is 'Immutability'?

options.text: Data cannot be changed once created.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `tap` or `inspect` function in pipelines?

options.text: Perform side effects without modifying data.

questions.questionText: What is the 'Maybe' (or 'Option') monad used for?
What is 'Higher-Order Function'?

options.text: A function that takes/returns a function.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `map` and `flatMap`?

options.text: `flatMap` flattens one level of nesting and maps.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Predicate' function?

options.text: A function that returns a boolean.
A function that returns a Promise.

questions.questionText: Which library is most famous for functional programming utilities in JS?

title: Frontend Architecture & Design Patterns
description: Design systems, module federation, and scalable project structure.

questions.questionText: What is the primary goal of 'Design Tokens'?

options.text: Platform-agnostic variables for design decisions (colors, spacing).

questions.questionText: What is 'Module Federation' (Webpack 5)?

options.text: Sharing code and dependencies across separate builds/deployments.
A way to organize Git submodules.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Micro Frontend'?

options.text: Breaking a frontend app into smaller, independently deliverable features.

questions.questionText: What is the 'Container/Presentational' pattern?

options.text: Separating state logic from UI rendering.

questions.questionText: What is the key principle of 'Atomic Design'?

options.text: Building UIs from atoms -> molecules -> organisms -> templates.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of a 'Monorepo' (e.g., Turborepo, Nx)?

options.text: Shared tooling, atomic commits across projects, and easier refactoring.

questions.questionText: What is 'Feature-Sliced Design' methodology?

options.text: Organizing code by business features and layers.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of an 'Error Boundary'?

options.text: Preventing entire UI crashes from isolated component errors.

questions.questionText: What is the dependency inversion principle in React context?

options.text: Components should depend on abstractions (props/callbacks) not concretions.

questions.questionText: Which pattern is used to avoid 'Prop Drilling'?
What is a 'Custom Hook' primarily meant for?
What is the Singleton pattern in JavaScript?

options.text: Ensuring a class has only one instance and provides global access.
A module that only exports one variable.

questions.questionText: What is 'Tree Shaking' in bundlers?

options.text: Eliminating dead code based on ES module static analysis.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Headless Component' pattern?

options.text: Providing logic and state without rendering UI (inversion of control).

questions.questionText: What is the advantage of 'Server Components' for architecture?

options.text: Zero-bundle-size components and direct backend access.

title: Database Design: SQL vs NoSQL
description: Queries, indexing, normalization, and choosing the right DB for the job.

questions.questionText: What does ACID stand for in database transactions?
What is the difference between `INNER JOIN` and `LEFT JOIN`?

options.text: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from left table even if no match.

questions.questionText: When is a NoSQL document database like MongoDB preferable over SQL?
What is an 'Index' in a database context?

options.text: Data structure to improve speed of data retrieval.

questions.questionText: What is 'Normalization' in SQL databases?

options.text: Organizing data to reduce redundancy and improve integrity.
Increasing query speed by duplicating data.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of a 'Foreign Key'?
What is 'Sharding'?

options.text: Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple servers.

questions.questionText: What is the CAP theorem?
What is a 'Redis' commonly used for?
What does 'ORM' stand for and provide?

options.text: Object-Relational Mapping; maps database rows to objects.

questions.questionText: What is an important consideration when using `SELECT *` in production?

options.text: Returning unnecessary columns wastes bandwidth and slows queries.
It's the fastest way to query data.
It ensures schema changes don't break app.

questions.questionText: What is 'Eventual Consistency'?

options.text: Data will become consistent across nodes over time.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Materialized View'?

options.text: A pre-computed stored result of a query.
A view that uses real data.

questions.questionText: What does the `EXPLAIN` command (or `EXPLAIN ANALYZE`) do?

options.text: Shows the query execution plan and cost.
Runs the query and explains errors.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `char` and `varchar` in SQL?

options.text: CHAR is fixed-length padded; VARCHAR is variable-length.
CHAR is only for numeric strings.

title: Progressive Web Apps & Real-time Communication
description: Service Workers, WebSockets, and building native-like web experiences.

questions.questionText: What is a core requirement for a Progressive Web App (PWA)?

options.text: Served over HTTPS and includes a Web App Manifest.

questions.questionText: What is the role of a 'Service Worker'?

options.text: Acts as a programmable network proxy to enable offline support.
Runs background sync for data uploads only.
Manages the DOM in the background.

questions.questionText: Which caching strategy returns the cached resource while fetching an update in the background?
How does WebSocket differ from HTTP?

options.text: Full-duplex, persistent connection with lower overhead.

questions.questionText: What is 'Server-Sent Events' (SSE)?

options.text: A standard allowing servers to push data to clients over HTTP.
A WebSocket alternative with bidirectional support.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of 'IndexedDB'?

options.text: Client-side storage for significant amounts of structured data.

questions.questionText: What is 'Web Push Notification'?

options.text: Browser notification triggered by server even when site is closed.

questions.questionText: What library is most commonly used for real-time bidirectional communication in Node.js?
What is the 'App Shell' model?

options.text: Caching the minimal HTML/CSS/JS required for UI structure to load instantly.
A design pattern for CLI apps.

questions.questionText: What is 'Background Sync' API?

options.text: Deferring actions until the user has stable connectivity.
Refreshing the page in the background.

questions.questionText: Which manifest property defines the full-screen display mode?
What is a 'Long-Polling' technique?

options.text: Client requests data and server waits to respond until data is available.
Keeping a TCP connection open forever.

questions.questionText: What does the `rel="manifest"` link tag do?

options.text: Links to the Web App Manifest file.

questions.questionText: Which scope can a Service Worker control?

options.text: The path where the service worker script is located and below.

questions.questionText: What is the state of WebSockets when the browser tab is in background (throttled)?

options.text: Messages are still received, but timers may be throttled.
Data is queued and delivered only on focus.

title: Web Accessibility (WCAG) & ARIA
description: Building inclusive web applications conforming to WCAG standards.

questions.questionText: What does `alt` text serve for images?

options.text: Provides a text equivalent for screen readers.
Fetches the image description from server.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `aria-label`?

options.text: Provides an accessible name for an element.

questions.questionText: What is the correct semantic HTML for page navigation?
What minimum contrast ratio does WCAG 2.1 AA require for normal text?
What is the correct use of `role="button"`?

options.text: Only if the element behaves like a button but isn't `<button>`.
It's required for all clickable elements.

questions.questionText: What is 'Focus Trapping'?

options.text: Keeping keyboard focus within a modal dialog.

questions.questionText: What does `aria-hidden="true"` do?

options.text: Hides the element from the accessibility tree.
Removes the element from the DOM.

questions.questionText: Why should you avoid using `outline: 0` or `outline: none` without replacing it?

options.text: Removes the visible focus indicator for keyboard users.
It's not supported by modern browsers.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `aria-labelledby` and `aria-describedby`?

options.text: Labelledby provides the name; describedby provides extra info.

questions.questionText: Which element is inherently a 'Landmark' that helps navigation?
What is the purpose of `lang` attribute on `<html>`?

options.text: Helps screen readers use correct pronunciation profile.

questions.questionText: How should you convey error messages in forms accessibly?

options.text: Using `aria-describedby` linking input to error container.

questions.questionText: What does the `sr-only` (screen reader only) class do?

options.text: Visually hides content but keeps it available for assistive tech.

questions.questionText: What is 'Skip Navigation' link?

options.text: First focusable link to bypass repeated header/menu content.
A link that opens in a new tab.

questions.questionText: What is the recommended way to implement a tabbed interface (Tabs)?

title: DevOps & Cloud Services Overview
description: Essential knowledge of cloud providers, IaC, and deployment strategies for developers.

questions.questionText: What does 'Infrastructure as Code' (IaC) allow teams to do?

options.text: Manage and provision infrastructure through machine-readable definition files.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of a 'Load Balancer'?

options.text: Distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers.

questions.questionText: What is an 'S3 Bucket' (AWS) / 'Blob Storage' (Azure) used for?
What is 'Serverless Computing' (e.g., AWS Lambda)?

options.text: Running code without managing underlying servers.

questions.questionText: What is a 'CDN' (Content Delivery Network)?

options.text: A distributed network of servers to deliver content faster based on geography.

questions.questionText: What is 'Horizontal Scaling' vs 'Vertical Scaling'?

options.text: Horizontal: adding more machines; Vertical: adding more power to existing machine.
Horizontal: scaling UI horizontally; Vertical: scrolling.
Horizontal: upgrading RAM; Vertical: adding nodes.

questions.questionText: What is the role of 'Terraform'?
What is a 'Reverse Proxy' (e.g., Nginx, Traefik)?

options.text: Server that sits in front of web servers and forwards client requests.
Proxy that hides client IP address.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Cloud Native' application generally characterized by?
What does 'High Availability' (HA) architecture aim for?
What is a 'Managed Database' service (e.g., RDS, Cloud SQL)?

options.text: Database where cloud provider handles backups, patching, replication.

questions.questionText: What is 'Auto Scaling'?

options.text: Automatically increasing/decreasing compute resources based on demand.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of using 'Environment Variables' for config?

options.text: They are faster to access than files.
They can be committed to Git.

questions.questionText: What is the `Dockerfile` instruction to set an environment variable?
What is the purpose of 'Health Checks' in orchestration (K8s)?

options.text: To determine if a container is ready/running to receive traffic.
To check if user has a gym membership.

title: Backend for Frontend (BFF) & API Composition
description: Patterns for tailoring backend services to specific frontend clients.

questions.questionText: What is the primary reason for implementing a 'Backend for Frontend' (BFF) layer?

options.text: To aggregate/filter data for specific UI clients (Mobile vs Web).
To replace the main backend database.

questions.questionText: How does GraphQL natively act as a BFF?

options.text: It provides a single endpoint where clients specify exact data needs.
It requires a separate BFF server.
It reduces data over-fetching but requires multiple endpoints.

questions.questionText: What is a key challenge of maintaining multiple BFFs (Mobile, Web, TV)?

options.text: Logic duplication and consistency across BFFs.

questions.questionText: Which issue does 'API Composition' solve in microservices?

options.text: Joining data from multiple services for a single view.

questions.questionText: In a BFF pattern, who is typically responsible for transforming REST responses to GraphQL?
What is the relationship between BFF and 'API Gateway'?

options.text: API Gateway handles routing/auth; BFF handles client-specific data shaping.
API Gateway is for internal traffic only.

questions.questionText: What is 'Federated GraphQL'?

options.text: Composing multiple GraphQL services into a single unified graph.
A political alliance of GraphQL companies.
Splitting the GraphQL schema into files.

questions.questionText: What is 'DataLoader' used for in a BFF?

options.text: Batching and caching database/API requests to prevent N+1 problem.

questions.questionText: What is a key benefit of using Node.js for BFF development?

options.text: Language alignment with frontend teams (TypeScript reuse).

questions.questionText: When developing a mobile BFF, what might you optimize differently than for web?

options.text: Fewer fields per request and bundled responses (less roundtrips).

questions.questionText: What does the 'Strangler Fig' pattern describe?

options.text: Gradually replacing a legacy monolith with new services/BFFs.
A way to prune dead CSS.

questions.questionText: What is the main risk of exposing raw downstream microservice errors directly via BFF?

options.text: Leaking internal implementation details and lack of abstraction.

questions.questionText: Which layer is responsible for converting timestamps to user-local timezone in BFF?
What is 'Clientside Schema Stitching'?

options.text: Combining multiple GraphQL endpoints on the frontend (less common than server-side).

questions.questionText: Why is 'Observability' (tracing headers) crucial in a BFF?

options.text: To correlate requests across microservices and BFF for debugging latency.

title: Node.js ORMs: Prisma & TypeORM
description: Database access patterns, migrations, and type safety in TypeScript backends.

questions.questionText: What is the primary advantage of using an ORM like Prisma over raw SQL queries?

options.text: Type safety, auto-completion, and easier migrations.
Eliminates the need for a database.

questions.questionText: What is `schema.prisma` file used for?

options.text: Defining the data model, generator, and datasource.

questions.questionText: What command is used to apply schema changes to the database in Prisma?

options.text: Model classes that contain both data and behavior (save, remove).
A way to track records historically.

questions.questionText: What is 'Data Mapper' pattern in TypeORM?

options.text: Repositories manage entity persistence; entity is a plain object.

questions.questionText: How do you define a one-to-many relation in Prisma schema?

options.text: The auto-generated query builder to interact with the database.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `include` in Prisma queries?

options.text: Serverless functions can exhaust DB connections quickly without it.

questions.questionText: What does `prisma generate` do?

options.text: Creates the Prisma Client based on the schema.

questions.questionText: In TypeORM, what does a 'Migration' do?

options.text: Version control for database schema changes.
Move data from SQL to NoSQL.

questions.questionText: How can you perform a raw SQL query with Prisma?
What is a 'Soft Delete' pattern?

options.text: Marking a record as deleted (`deletedAt` column) instead of removing row.
Deleting records in a background job.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of using an `enum` defined in Prisma schema?

options.text: Type-safe constraints on column values in DB and generated Types.

questions.questionText: What is 'Prisma Accelerate' or 'Data Proxy' used for?

options.text: Connection pooling and caching for serverless environments.

title: System Design for Developers
description: Short-link services, chat systems, and scalable architecture concepts.

options.text: Base64 Encoding of an MD5 hash

questions.questionText: Why is a Bloom Filter used in a URL shortener or caching layer?

options.text: To check if a short URL exists in DB with low memory footprint.

questions.questionText: In a chat system, what is WebSocket used for primarily?

options.text: Real-time message delivery and typing indicators.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of a 'Message Queue' (Kafka/RabbitMQ) in microservices?

options.text: Decoupling services and ensuring reliable async processing.

questions.questionText: Which database type is preferred for storing millions of relationships (social graph)?

options.text: Reducing latency and origin server load.

questions.questionText: What does 'Consistent Hashing' solve?

options.text: Minimizing key redistribution when adding/removing cache nodes.

questions.questionText: What is 'Write-Ahead Logging' (WAL) used for in databases?

Writing SQL queries ahead of time.

questions.questionText: What is the advantage of 'Read Replicas' in SQL databases?

options.text: Distribute read traffic to scale performance.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between 'Latency' and 'Throughput'?

Latency is network speed; Throughput is CPU speed.
Latency is upload; Throughput is download.

questions.questionText: What is 'Event Sourcing'?

questions.questionText: What is a 'Rate Limiter' algorithm?

options.text: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window Log.

questions.questionText: In a Distributed System, what is a 'Split Brain' scenario?

options.text: Network partition where two subsets think they are the leader.
Two developers working on same file.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of 'Heartbeat' mechanism?

questions.questionText: What does 'Idempotency' guarantee in REST API design?

options.text: Multiple identical requests have same side-effect as a single request.

title: React Native Fundamentals

questions.questionText: What is the core difference between React Native and React JS?

React Native uses a different language.

React JS supports hooks; Native does not.

questions.questionText: How can you persist data locally in React Native securely?

title: Debugging, Logging & Performance Monitoring

questions.questionText: What is the most common use of `console.trace()`?

questions.questionText: What is a 'Source Map'?

options.text: File mapping minified/transpiled code back to original source.
A file showing dependencies of a module.

questions.questionText: What is the goal of 'Application Performance Monitoring' (APM) tools like Datadog, New Relic?

options.text: Tracking response times, error rates, and system health.

questions.questionText: What is 'Sentry' mainly used for?

options.text: Real-time error tracking and crash reporting.

questions.questionText: What is 'Structured Logging'?

questions.questionText: What is the 'Redux DevTools' used for?

questions.questionText: What does `node --inspect` allow?

questions.questionText: What is a common method to detect memory leaks in React?
What is 'Lighthouse' in Chrome DevTools?

options.text: Tool for auditing performance, accessibility, SEO.

questions.questionText: How can you simulate slow network conditions in Chrome?

options.text: Scripted simulations of user flows to check availability.
Monitoring based on real user metrics.
Check for synthetic fibers in UI.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of 'Correlation IDs'?

questions.questionText: What is an 'Uncontrolled Component' memory leak?

title: SEO & SSR Strategies for SPAs
description: Optimizing JavaScript applications for search engines and social sharing.

questions.questionText: What is the main SEO challenge with traditional Client-Side Rendered (CSR) React apps?

options.text: Search engine bots might not wait for JavaScript to render content.

questions.questionText: How does Next.js `getServerSideProps` help with SEO?

options.text: It renders the page with data on the server, delivering fully populated HTML.
It injects meta tags via middleware.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the `robots.txt` file?

options.text: Instructing search engine crawlers which URLs to access.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Canonical URL'?

options.text: Preferred URL for duplicate or similar pages.

questions.questionText: What are 'Open Graph' meta tags used for?

options.text: Controlling how content appears when shared on social media.

questions.questionText: What is 'Incremental Static Regeneration' (ISR) benefit for SEO?

options.text: Static pages served fast while being updatable without full redeploy.

questions.questionText: What is the function of `<meta name='description'>`?

options.text: Provides summary snippet for search engine results.

questions.questionText: Why is 'Largest Contentful Paint' (LCP) an SEO ranking factor?

options.text: Google prioritizes pages with good user experience (fast load).
It measures the size of images.
It checks for valid SSL certs.

questions.questionText: What is 'Structured Data' (JSON-LD) used for?

options.text: Providing explicit clues about page meaning to search engines (Rich Snippets).

questions.questionText: What is the `nofollow` attribute for?

options.text: Telling search engines not to pass link equity.

questions.questionText: What is the benefit of 'Server Components' for SEO?

options.text: Zero JS sent to client for static content; faster HTML streaming.

questions.questionText: What is 'Prerendering'?
What should the HTTP status code be for a soft 404 page?
What does 'Cumulative Layout Shift' refer to?

options.text: Visual stability metric measuring unexpected movement of elements.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of `next/head`?

options.text: Modifying the HTML head (title, meta) per page.

title: Clean Code & SOLID Principles in JS/TS
description: Applying software design principles to write maintainable frontend/backend code.

questions.questionText: What does the 'S' in SOLID stand for?
What does the 'O' in SOLID stand for?
What does 'Liskov Substitution Principle' (L) state?

options.text: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types.
A class should have only one instance.

questions.questionText: What is 'Dependency Inversion Principle' (D)?

options.text: High-level modules shouldn't depend on low-level modules; both depend on abstractions.

questions.questionText: What is 'DRY'?
What is 'KISS' principle?
What is 'YAGNI'?
What is 'Function Composition'?

options.text: Pipe output of one function to input of another.

questions.questionText: What does 'Law of Demeter' suggest?

options.text: A unit should talk only to its immediate friends.
Functions should only change global state.

questions.questionText: What is 'Cyclomatic Complexity'?

options.text: Measure of code complexity based on branches.
Number of files in a directory.

questions.questionText: What is 'Feature Envy' code smell?

options.text: A method uses more data from another class than its own.

questions.questionText: What does 'Interface Segregation Principle' (I) state?

options.text: Clients shouldn't depend on interfaces they don't use.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Pure Function'?

options.text: Same input always same output; no side effects.

questions.questionText: What is 'Magic Number' anti-pattern?

options.text: Using hardcoded literal values without explanation.

questions.questionText: What is 'Guard Clause'?

options.text: Early return to handle invalid/edge conditions.

title: Advanced Git Scenarios & Collaboration
description: Solving complex merge conflicts, reflog recovery, and advanced workflows.

questions.questionText: What does `git reset --hard HEAD~1` do?

options.text: Destroys the last commit and changes in working directory.
Unstages the last commit but keeps changes.
Creates a new commit reversing the last one.

questions.questionText: How can you recover a deleted branch that wasn't merged?

options.text: Use `git reflog` to find the last commit SHA and create a new branch.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `git merge` and `git rebase` onto main?

options.text: Merge preserves history graph; rebase rewrites commits linearly.
Merge is only for remote branches.

questions.questionText: What does `git cherry-pick` do?

options.text: Apply specific commit from another branch to current branch.

questions.questionText: What is the 'HEAD' pointer?

options.text: Reference to the currently checked out commit/branch.
The first commit in the array.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Detached HEAD' state?

options.text: HEAD points directly to a commit SHA, not a branch.

questions.questionText: What is the 'Stash' used for?
What does `git bisect` do?

options.text: Binary search through commit history to find a bug.

questions.questionText: What is the effect of `git push --force-with-lease`?

options.text: Safer force push ensuring remote ref hasn't been updated by others.
Force push with lease on file locks.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between `^` and `~` in commit references?
How do you rename a branch?
What does `git rebase -i` (interactive) allow?

options.text: Squash, reword, edit, drop, reorder commits.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Submodule'?

options.text: Embedding another Git repository inside a folder.

questions.questionText: What is 'Git Flow'?

options.text: Branching model with main, develop, feature, release, hotfix.

questions.questionText: What is a 'Merge Conflict'?

options.text: Occurs when Git cannot automatically resolve differences in code.
A bug in the GitHub UI.
